title.zh: 卷三十七 季布欒布田叔傳
title.en: Volume 37: Ji Bu, Luan Bu and Tian Shu
title.raw: 卷三十七 季布欒布田叔傳 Volume 37: Ji Bu, Luan Bu and Tian Shu

sentences.zh: 季布，楚人也，為任俠有名。

translations.literal: Ji Bu was a man of Chu; as one who took on obligations of knightly righteousness he was famous.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was from Chu and had won renown as a knight-errant who stood by his sworn obligations.

sentences.zh: 項籍使將兵，數窘漢王。

translations.literal: Xiang Ji had him lead troops, and he repeatedly placed the King of Han in straits.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu put him in command of troops, and time and again he cornered the King of Han.

sentences.zh: 項籍滅，高祖購求布千金，敢有舍匿，罪三族。

translations.literal: When Xiang Ji was destroyed, Gaozu offered a thousand catties of gold for Ji Bu; whoever dared harbor or conceal him would be guilty of the three-clan punishment.
translations.idiomatic: After Xiang Yu fell, Gaozu posted a bounty of a thousand pounds of gold for Ji Bu, with death for the offender’s whole extended family if anyone sheltered him.

sentences.zh: 布匿濮陽周氏，周氏曰：「漢求將軍急，跡且至臣家，能聽臣，臣敢進計；

translations.literal: Bu hid with the Zhou clan of Puyang. The Zhou said: “The Han search for the general is urgent; the trail will soon reach my house. If you can heed me, I dare advance a plan;
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu took refuge with the Zhou family of Puyang. The head of the family told him, “The court is hunting you relentlessly, and their agents will soon be at my door. If you will trust me, I have a plan;

sentences.zh: 即否，願先自剄。」

translations.literal: if not, I wish first to cut my own throat.”
translations.idiomatic: if not, I would rather slit my own throat first.”

sentences.zh: 布許之。

translations.literal: Bu assented.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu agreed.

sentences.zh: 乃髡鉗布，衣褐，置廣柳車中，并與其家僮數十人，之魯朱家所賣之。

translations.literal: Thereupon they shaved his head and put the iron collar on Bu, clothed him in coarse hemp, placed him in a broad-willow cart, together with several tens of his household slaves, and went to the Lu house of Zhu Jia and sold him.
translations.idiomatic: They then shaved his head and collared him like a convict, dressed him in a poor man’s cloak, hid him in a rough transport wagon along with dozens of family retainers, and sold the whole lot to Zhu Jia of Lu.

sentences.zh: 朱家心知其季布也，買置田舍。

translations.literal: Zhu Jia in his heart knew him to be Ji Bu; he bought him and placed him in field and house.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Jia recognized him at once as Ji Bu, bought him, and installed him on his estate.

sentences.zh: 乃之雒陽見汝陰侯滕公，說曰：「季布何罪？

translations.literal: Then he went to Luoyang and saw the Marquis of Ruoyin, Lord Teng, and urged: “What crime has Ji Bu?
translations.idiomatic: He then traveled to Luoyang to see Xiahou Ying, the Marquis of Ruoyin, and argued, “What wrong has Ji Bu done?

sentences.zh: 臣各為其主用，職耳。

translations.literal: Ministers each served their own lord; it was duty, that is all.
translations.idiomatic: Each minister served his own lord—that was simply his duty.

sentences.zh: 項氏臣豈可盡誅邪？

translations.literal: Could all the ministers of the Xiang clan be executed to the last?
translations.idiomatic: You cannot execute every last minister who once served the house of Xiang.

sentences.zh: 今上始得天下，而以私怨求一人，何示不廣也！

translations.literal: Now the sovereign has just obtained the empire, yet by private resentment seeks one man—what a narrow signal that sends!
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven has only just won the realm; to hound one man out of personal spite shows a remarkably small mind.

sentences.zh: 且以季布之賢，漢求之急如此，此不北走胡，南走越耳。

translations.literal: Moreover, given Ji Bu’s worth, if the Han presses the search this urgently, he will not flee north to the Hu or south to Yue for nothing.
translations.idiomatic: Besides, Ji Bu is a man of real ability; push him this hard and he will end up in the Xiongnu’s camp to the north or Nanyue to the south.

sentences.zh: 夫忌壯士以資敵國，此伍子胥所以鞭荊平之墓也。

translations.literal: To grudge a brave man and thereby strengthen a rival state—this is why Wu Zixu whipped the tomb of King Ping of Jing.
translations.idiomatic: Driving a formidable warrior into the enemy’s arms is how you get another King Ping of Chu and another Wu Zixu desecrating royal tombs.

sentences.zh: 君何不從容為上言之？」

translations.literal: Why does my lord not at leisure speak to the sovereign of this?”
translations.idiomatic: Why not find a quiet moment and put this to His Majesty yourself?”

sentences.zh: 滕公心知朱家大俠，意布匿其所，乃許諾。

translations.literal: Lord Teng in his heart knew Zhu Jia was a great knight-errant; he suspected Bu was hidden with him, and thereupon promised.
translations.idiomatic: Xiahou Ying knew Zhu Jia’s reputation as a great knight-errant and guessed Ji Bu lay hidden on his lands; he agreed to speak.

sentences.zh: 侍間，果言如朱家指。

translations.literal: Attending during an interval, he indeed spoke as Zhu Jia had directed.
translations.idiomatic: When he next attended the emperor, he made the case exactly as Zhu Jia had outlined.

sentences.zh: 上乃赦布。

translations.literal: The sovereign thereupon pardoned Bu.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then pardoned Ji Bu.

sentences.zh: 當是時，諸公皆多布能摧剛為柔，朱家亦以此名聞當世。

translations.literal: At that time the great men all praised Bu for being able to grind rigidity into pliancy; Zhu Jia also by this had his name heard in that age.
translations.idiomatic: At the time, leading figures admired Ji Bu for learning to bend when he could not break, and Zhu Jia’s name rang through the age for it.

sentences.zh: 布召見，謝，拜郎中。

translations.literal: Bu was summoned and seen; he apologized and was appointed gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was summoned to audience, thanked the throne for his life, and was appointed a gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: 孝惠時，為中郎將。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaohui, he was general of the gentlemen of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Hui he served as general of the gentlemen of the palace.

sentences.zh: 單于嘗為書嫚呂太后，太后怒，召諸將議之。

translations.literal: The Chanyu once wrote a letter insulting Empress Dowager Lü; the empress dowager was angry and summoned the generals to deliberate.
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu had once sent a letter that insulted Empress Dowager Lü; enraged, she called in her generals to discuss a response.

sentences.zh: 上將軍樊噲曰：「臣願得十萬眾，橫行匈奴中。」

translations.literal: General-in-chief Fan Kuai said: “Your servant wishes to obtain a hundred thousand men and sweep through the midst of the Xiongnu.”
translations.idiomatic: Fan Kuai, the senior general, declared, “Give me a hundred thousand men and I will drive straight through the heart of the Xiongnu steppe.”

sentences.zh: 諸將皆阿呂太后，以噲言為然。

translations.literal: The generals all fawned on Empress Dowager Lü and took Kuai’s words as right.
translations.idiomatic: The other generals curried favor with the empress dowager and echoed Fan Kuai’s bravado.

sentences.zh: 布曰：「樊噲可斬也。

translations.literal: Bu said: “Fan Kuai may be beheaded.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu said, “Fan Kuai deserves execution.

sentences.zh: 夫以高帝兵三十餘萬，困於平城，噲時亦在其中。

translations.literal: For with Gaodi’s army of more than three hundred thousand trapped at Pingcheng, Kuai at that time was also among them.
translations.idiomatic: When Gaodi’s three hundred thousand troops were trapped at Pingcheng, Fan Kuai was right there in the ranks.

sentences.zh: 今噲奈何以十萬眾橫行匈奴中，面謾！

translations.literal: Now how can Kuai with a hundred thousand men sweep through the midst of the Xiongnu—lying to the face!
translations.idiomatic: How dare he now boast that a mere hundred thousand could overrun the Xiongnu heartland? He is lying to your faces!

sentences.zh: 且秦以事胡，陳勝等起。

translations.literal: Moreover, Qin because of affairs with the Hu, Chen Sheng and others arose.
translations.idiomatic: Remember that the Qin empire cracked in rebellion when it threw everything into war on the northern frontier.

sentences.zh: 今瘡痍未瘳，噲又面諛，欲搖動天下。」

translations.literal: Now the wounds are not yet healed, and Kuai again flatters to the face, wishing to shake the realm.”
translations.idiomatic: The realm is still bleeding from those wounds, and Fan Kuai flatters you to your faces and would set the world trembling again.”

sentences.zh: 是時殿上皆恐，太后罷朝，遂不復議擊匈奴事。

translations.literal: At that time those on the hall all feared; the empress dowager ended court and thereupon did not again deliberate attacking the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: The courtiers on the dais were stricken with fear; the empress dowager cut short the session and never again brought up striking the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 布為河東守。

translations.literal: Bu was governor of Hedong.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was appointed governor of Hedong.

sentences.zh: 孝文時，人有言其賢，召欲以為御史大夫。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaowen, someone spoke of his worth; he was summoned, desiring to make him imperial clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Wen someone praised his talent, and the court summoned him intending to name him imperial clerk.

sentences.zh: 人又言其勇，使酒難近。

translations.literal: Someone again spoke of his courage, that when drunk he was hard to approach.
translations.idiomatic: Others spoke of his fierceness and his bullying ways when drunk, warning that he was difficult to work with.

sentences.zh: 至，留邸一月，見罷。

translations.literal: When he arrived, he was detained in the lodge a month and dismissed after audience.
translations.idiomatic: When he reached the capital he was kept waiting a month in the official hostel and then sent home without the post.

sentences.zh: 布進曰：「臣待罪河東，陛下無故召臣，此人必有以臣欺陛下者。

translations.literal: Bu advanced and said: “Your servant awaits punishment at Hedong; the sovereign for no reason summoned your servant—this person must have had something by which to deceive the sovereign.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu stepped forward and said, “I was serving at Hedong when Your Majesty summoned me without cause—someone must have oversold me to you.

sentences.zh: 今臣至，無所受事，罷去，此人必有毀臣者。

translations.literal: Now your servant has arrived, received no assignment, and been dismissed to go—this person must have had one who slanders your servant.
translations.idiomatic: Now that I am here you give me no charge and send me away—someone has clearly blackened my name.

sentences.zh: 夫陛下以一人譽召臣，一人毀去臣，臣恐天下有識者聞之，有以窺陛下。」

translations.literal: For the sovereign by one man’s praise summoned your servant, by one man’s slander sent your servant away; your servant fears that those in the realm with understanding who hear it will thereby have means to take the sovereign’s measure.”
translations.idiomatic: If one man’s praise brings me to court and one man’s slander sends me packing, every thoughtful subject in the realm will know how easily Your Majesty can be swayed.”

sentences.zh: 上默然，慚曰：「河東吾股肱郡，故特召君耳。」

translations.literal: The sovereign was silent, ashamed, and said: “Hedong is my thigh-and-arm commandery; therefore I specially summoned you, that is all.”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor fell silent, then said with embarrassment, “Hedong is one of my vital provinces; I simply wanted a look at you.”

sentences.zh: 布之官。

translations.literal: Bu went to his office.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu returned to his post.

sentences.zh: 辯士曹丘生數招權顧金錢，事貴人趙談等，與竇長君善。

translations.literal: The rhetorician Cao Qiusheng repeatedly curried power and looked to money, served the noble Zhao Tan and others, and was on good terms with Dou Changjun.
translations.idiomatic: The persuader Cao Qiusheng traded on connections for cash, flattered powerful men such as Zhao Tan, and cultivated Dou Changjun.

sentences.zh: 布聞，寄書諫長君曰：「吾聞曹丘生非長者，勿與通。」

translations.literal: Bu heard, sent a letter remonstrating Changjun: “I have heard Cao Qiusheng is not an elder; do not communicate with him.”
translations.idiomatic: When Ji Bu heard of it he wrote Dou Changjun a warning: “Cao Qiusheng is no gentleman—have nothing to do with him.”

sentences.zh: 及曹丘生歸，欲得書請布。

translations.literal: When Cao Qiusheng returned, he wished to obtain a letter and request Bu.
translations.idiomatic: When Cao Qiusheng came home he wanted a letter of introduction so he could call on Ji Bu.

sentences.zh: 竇長君曰：「季將軍不說足下，足下無往。」

translations.literal: Dou Changjun said: “General Ji is not pleased with you; do not go.”
translations.idiomatic: Dou Changjun said, “General Ji dislikes you; you had better not go.”

sentences.zh: 固請書，遂行。

translations.literal: He firmly requested a letter, and thereupon went.
translations.idiomatic: Cao pressed until Dou gave him a letter, then set out.

sentences.zh: 使人先發書，布果大怒，待曹丘。

translations.literal: He had someone first send the letter; Bu indeed was greatly angry and awaited Cao Qiu.
translations.idiomatic: He sent a messenger ahead with the letter; Ji Bu flew into a rage and waited for Cao Qiusheng.

sentences.zh: 曹丘至，則揖布曰：「楚人諺曰『得黃金百，不如得季布諾』，足下何以得此聲梁楚之間哉？

translations.literal: When Cao Qiu arrived, he bowed to Bu and said: “The people of Chu have a proverb, ‘Better a hundred catties of yellow gold than obtaining Ji Bu’s promise’—how did you obtain this reputation in the space between Liang and Chu?
translations.idiomatic: When Cao Qiusheng arrived he bowed and said, “Chu has a saying: ‘A hundred catties of gold cannot buy Ji Bu’s word’—how did you earn that reputation all the way from Liang to Chu?

sentences.zh: 且僕與足下俱楚人，使僕游揚足下名於天下，顧不美乎？

translations.literal: Moreover your servant and you are both men of Chu; were your servant to spread your name through the realm, would it not be fine?
translations.idiomatic: We are both sons of Chu; if I trumpet your name across the empire, would that not serve you well?

sentences.zh: 何足下距僕之深也！」

translations.literal: Why do you keep your servant at such a distance!”
translations.idiomatic: Why do you shut me out so coldly!”

sentences.zh: 布乃大說。

translations.literal: Bu thereupon was greatly pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was delighted.

sentences.zh: 引入，留數月，為上客，厚送之。

translations.literal: He led him in, retained him several months, treated him as a honored guest, and sent him off generously.
translations.idiomatic: He brought him inside, kept him for months as an honored guest, and sent him away laden with gifts.

sentences.zh: 布名所以益聞者，曹丘揚之也。

translations.literal: The reason Bu’s name thereby became more heard was that Cao Qiu spread it.
translations.idiomatic: It was Cao Qiusheng’s praise that made Ji Bu’s name ring louder still.

sentences.zh: 布弟季心氣蓋關中，遇人恭謹，為任俠，方數千里，士爭為死。

translations.literal: Bu’s younger brother Ji Xin’s spirit covered Guanzhong; encountering people he was respectful and careful; as a knight-errant, within several thousand li, knights vied to die for him.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu’s younger brother Ji Xin’s reputation overshadowed Guanzhong; though courteous to everyone he met, as a knight-errant his name drew willing followers for a thousand miles around.

sentences.zh: 嘗殺人，亡吳，從爰絲匿，長事爰絲，弟畜灌夫、籍福之屬。

translations.literal: He once killed a man, fled to Wu, followed Aisi and hid, in length served Aisi as elder, as younger brother raised Guan Fu, Ji Fu, and their sort.
translations.idiomatic: He had once killed a man and fled to Wu, where he hid with Yuan Ang, served him with the deference of a junior, and treated Guan Fu and Ji Fu as younger brothers.

sentences.zh: 嘗為中司馬，中尉郅都不敢加。

translations.literal: He once was marshal of the center; Commandant Zhi Du did not dare touch him.
translations.idiomatic: As marshal of the center he was a man even the dreaded Commandant Zhi Du would not provoke.

sentences.zh: 少年多時時竊借其名以行。

translations.literal: The youths often secretly borrowed his name to act.
translations.idiomatic: Young ruffians constantly invoked his name to throw their weight around.

sentences.zh: 當是時，季心以勇，布以諾，聞關中。

translations.literal: At that time Ji Xin by courage, Bu by promise, were heard of in Guanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: At the time Guanzhong knew Ji Xin for daring and Ji Bu for keeping his word.

sentences.zh: 布母弟丁公，為項羽將，逐窘高祖彭城西。

translations.literal: Bu’s maternal younger brother Ding Gong was a general of Xiang Yu; he pursued and placed Gaozu in straits west of Pengcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu’s half-brother on his mother’s side, Ding Gong, served Xiang Yu as a general and once chased Gaozu into a corner west of Pengcheng.

sentences.zh: 短兵接，漢王急，顧謂丁公曰：「兩賢豈相厄哉！」

translations.literal: Short weapons joined; the King of Han was in straits; looking back he said to Ding Gong: “Can two worthy men afflict each other!”
translations.idiomatic: At close quarters the King of Han was desperate; glancing back he cried to Ding Gong, “Would two good men destroy each other?”

sentences.zh: 丁公引兵而還。

translations.literal: Ding Gong led the troops and returned.
translations.idiomatic: Ding Gong broke off the pursuit and withdrew his troops.

sentences.zh: 及項王滅，丁公謁見高祖，以丁公徇軍中，曰：「丁公為項王臣不忠，使項王失天下者也。」

translations.literal: When King Xiang was destroyed, Ding Gong went to see Gaozu; taking Ding Gong he displayed him through the army, saying: “Ding Gong was minister to King Xiang and disloyal; he is the one who made King Xiang lose the realm.”
translations.idiomatic: After Xiang Yu fell, Ding Gong presented himself to Gaozu, who paraded him before the host and declared, “Here is a minister of Xiang Yu who broke faith—the man who cost his king the empire.”

sentences.zh: 遂斬之，曰：「使後為人臣無傚丁公也！」

translations.literal: Thereupon he beheaded him, saying: “Let those later who are ministers to others not imitate Ding Gong!”
translations.idiomatic: He then had him beheaded, adding, “Let no future minister take Ding Gong as his model!”

sentences.zh: 欒布，梁人也。

translations.literal: Luan Bu was a man of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu was from Liang.

sentences.zh: 彭越為家人時，嘗與布游，窮困，賣庸於齊，為酒家保。

translations.literal: When Peng Yue was still a commoner, he once roamed with Bu; in poverty and straits he sold his labor in Qi and was a tavern guarantor.
translations.idiomatic: When Peng Yue was still a commoner he had traveled with Luan Bu; down and out, he hired himself out in Qi and worked as a potman in an inn.

sentences.zh: 數歲別去，而布為人所略，賣為奴於燕。

translations.literal: After several years they parted and left, while Bu was seized by someone and sold as a slave in Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Years later they parted ways, and Luan Bu was kidnapped and sold into slavery in Yan.

sentences.zh: 為其主家報仇，燕將臧荼舉以為都尉。

translations.literal: For his master’s household he avenged a wrong; the Yan general Zang Tu raised him and made him commandant.
translations.idiomatic: He avenged his master’s household, and the Yan general Zang Tu singled him out and appointed him commandant.

sentences.zh: 荼為燕王，布為將。

translations.literal: When Tu became King of Yan, Bu was a general.
translations.idiomatic: When Zang Tu became king of Yan, Luan Bu served as his general.

sentences.zh: 及荼反，漢擊燕，虜布。

translations.literal: When Tu rebelled, the Han struck Yan and captured Bu.
translations.idiomatic: When Zang Tu rebelled, Han forces attacked Yan and took Luan Bu prisoner.

sentences.zh: 梁王彭越聞之，乃言上，請贖布為梁大夫。

translations.literal: King Liang Peng Yue heard of it, then spoke to the sovereign, requesting to ransom Bu and make him a grandee of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: King Peng Yue of Liang heard of it and petitioned the emperor to ransom him and appoint him a grandee of Liang.

sentences.zh: 使於齊，未反，漢召彭越責以謀反，夷三族，梟首雒陽，下詔有收視者輒捕之。

translations.literal: He was sent on mission to Qi; before he returned, the Han summoned Peng Yue and charged him with plotting rebellion, exterminated the three clans, displayed the head at Luoyang, and issued an edict that whoever gathered or viewed the corpse would be seized at once.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu was on a mission to Qi when, before he could return, the court summoned Peng Yue, convicted him of treason, wiped out his kin to the third degree, spiked his head at Luoyang, and decreed death for anyone who so much as claimed the body.

sentences.zh: 布還，奏事彭越頭下，祠而哭之。

translations.literal: Bu returned, reported his business beneath Peng Yue’s head, sacrificed, and wept for him.
translations.idiomatic: When Luan Bu came back he presented his report beneath Peng Yue’s severed head, offered sacrifice, and wept.

sentences.zh: 吏捕以聞。

translations.literal: The clerks seized him and reported.
translations.idiomatic: Officials arrested him and reported it.

sentences.zh: 上召布罵曰：「若與彭越反邪？

translations.literal: The sovereign summoned Bu and reviled: “Did you plot rebellion with Peng Yue?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor had him brought in and snarled, “Were you in league with Peng Yue?

sentences.zh: 吾禁人勿收，若獨祠而哭之，與反明矣。

translations.literal: I forbade people not to gather him; you alone sacrificed and wept for him—with rebellion it is clear.”
translations.idiomatic: I forbade anyone to mourn him, yet you alone offered sacrifice and wept—that proves your guilt.”

sentences.zh: 趣亨之。」

translations.literal: Hasten to boil him.”
translations.idiomatic: Take him away and boil him alive.”

sentences.zh: 方提趨湯，顧曰：「願一言而死。」

translations.literal: Just as he was being lifted and hurried toward the cauldron, looking back he said: “I wish one word and then to die.”
translations.idiomatic: As they dragged him toward the boiling cauldron he looked back and said, “Let me speak one sentence before I die.”

sentences.zh: 上曰：「何言？」

translations.literal: The sovereign said: “What words?”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked, “What is it?”

sentences.zh: 布曰：「方上之困彭城，敗滎陽、成皋間，項王所以不能遂西，徒以彭王居梁地，與漢合從苦楚也。

translations.literal: Bu said: “When the sovereign was trapped at Pengcheng, defeated between Xingyang and Chenggao, the reason King Xiang could not thereupon press west was only because Peng Wang dwelt in Liang territory, with Han in vertical alliance and suffering Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu said, “When you were trapped at Pengcheng and driven from Xingyang to Chenggao, the only reason Xiang Yu never drove west was that Peng Yue held Liang in alliance with Han and pinned Chu’s forces.

sentences.zh: 當是之時，彭王壹顧，與楚則漢破，與漢則楚破。

translations.literal: At that time, if Peng Wang had turned one glance—if with Chu then Han was broken, if with Han then Chu was broken.
translations.idiomatic: At that moment a single shift of Peng Yue’s loyalty would have shattered Han had he joined Chu, or Chu had he joined Han.

sentences.zh: 且垓下之會，微彭王，項氏不亡。

translations.literal: Moreover, at the meeting below Gaixia, without Peng Wang, the Xiang clan would not have perished.
translations.idiomatic: At Gaixia, without Peng Yue, the house of Xiang would never have fallen.

sentences.zh: 天下已定，彭王剖符受封，亦欲傳之萬世。

translations.literal: When the realm was settled, Peng Wang received tallies and investiture; he also wished to transmit them ten thousand generations.
translations.idiomatic: When peace came, Peng Yue received his fief and bronze tallies like any other king, expecting his line to endure for ages.

sentences.zh: 今漢壹徵兵於梁，彭王病不行，而疑以為反。

translations.literal: Now the Han in one stroke conscripted troops in Liang; Peng Wang was ill and did not go, and by suspicion was deemed to have rebelled.
translations.idiomatic: Then Han called up every able man in Liang; Peng Yue was too ill to march, and on that slender ground you branded him a traitor.

sentences.zh: 反形未見，以苛細誅之，臣恐功臣人人自危也。

translations.literal: The form of rebellion had not appeared, yet by harsh minuteness he was executed; your servant fears that merit-holders will each in his own person stand in fear.
translations.idiomatic: No act of rebellion had appeared, yet you cut him down on petty pretexts—I fear every man who helped you win the throne now sleeps uneasy.

sentences.zh: 今彭王已死，臣生不如死，請就亨。」

translations.literal: Now Peng Wang is already dead; your servant to live is not as good as to die; I request to go to the boiling.”
translations.idiomatic: Peng Yue is dead; for me to live would be worse than death—send me to the cauldron.”

sentences.zh: 上乃釋布，拜為都尉。

translations.literal: The sovereign thereupon released Bu and appointed him commandant.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then spared him and named him commandant.

sentences.zh: 孝文時，為燕相，至將軍。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaowen, he was chancellor of Yan, reaching to general.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Wen he rose from chancellor of Yan to general.

sentences.zh: 布稱曰：「窮困不能辱身，非人也；

translations.literal: Bu declared: “In poverty and straits, if one cannot submit the body to humiliation, it is not to be human;
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu used to say, “A man who in hard times will not endure a little humiliation is no true man;

sentences.zh: 富貴不能快意，非賢也。」

translations.literal: in wealth and honor, if one cannot satisfy the will, it is not to be worthy.”
translations.idiomatic: a man who gains wealth and power yet cannot have his due satisfaction is no worthy man.”

sentences.zh: 於是嘗有德，厚報之；

translations.literal: Thereupon if he once had received a kindness, he repaid it thickly;
translations.idiomatic: Accordingly he repaid every kindness lavishly;

sentences.zh: 有怨，必以法滅之。

translations.literal: if he had a grudge, he was sure to destroy them by law.
translations.idiomatic: and every slight he settled through the courts.

sentences.zh: 吳楚反時，以功封為鄃侯，復為燕相。

translations.literal: When Wu and Chu rebelled, by merit he was enfeoffed as marquis of Yu, and again was chancellor of Yan.
translations.idiomatic: When Wu and Chu rose in revolt, his service won him the marquisate of Yu and a second term as Yan’s chancellor.

sentences.zh: 燕齊之間皆為立社，號曰欒公社。

translations.literal: Between Yan and Qi all for him established shrines, styled the shrines of Lord Luan.
translations.idiomatic: From Yan to Qi the people set up shrines in his honor and called them the altars of Lord Luan.

sentences.zh: 布薨，子賁嗣侯，孝武時坐為太常犧牲不如令，國除。

translations.literal: Bu died; his son Fen succeeded the marquis; in the time of Emperor Xiaowu he sat because as grand master of ceremonies the sacrificial victims did not accord with ordinance, and the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: When Luan Bu died his son Fen inherited the title; under Emperor Wu the fief was stripped after Fen, as grand master of ceremonies, let the ritual victims fall short of statute.

sentences.zh: 田叔，趙陘城人也。

translations.literal: Tian Shu was a man of Xingcheng in Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu came from Xingcheng in Zhao.

sentences.zh: 其先，齊田氏也。

translations.literal: His ancestors were the Tian clan of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: His forebears were the Tian lineage of Qi.

sentences.zh: 叔好劍，學黃老術於樂鉅公。

translations.literal: Shu loved the sword and studied Huang-Lao techniques with Master Yue Ju.
translations.idiomatic: He loved fencing and studied Huang–Lao doctrine under Master Yue Ju.

sentences.zh: 為人廉直，喜任俠。

translations.literal: As a man he was incorrupt and straight, and delighted in knightly righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: He was honest, upright, and drawn to the code of the knight-errant.

sentences.zh: 游諸公，趙人舉之趙相趙午，言之趙王張敖，以為郎中。

translations.literal: He roamed among the great men; the people of Zhao raised him to minister Zhao Wu of Zhao, who spoke of him to King Zhang Ao of Zhao, and he was made gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: He moved among Zhao’s notables until the people recommended him to Chancellor Zhao Wu, who introduced him to King Zhang Ao and had him appointed a gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: 數歲，趙王賢之，未及遷。

translations.literal: After several years the king of Zhao deemed him worthy, but before he had promoted him.
translations.idiomatic: The king came to admire him, though years passed without a promotion.

sentences.zh: 會趙午、貫高等謀弒上，事發覺，漢下詔捕趙王及群臣反者。

translations.literal: It happened that Zhao Wu, Guan Gao, and others plotted to assassinate the sovereign; the affair was discovered, and the Han issued an edict to seize the king of Zhao and the ministers who had rebelled.
translations.idiomatic: Then Zhao Wu, Guan Gao, and others plotted to kill the emperor; when the plot surfaced, Han issued warrants for King Zhang Ao and every minister implicated.

sentences.zh: 趙有敢隨王，罪三族。

translations.literal: In Zhao whoever dared follow the king would be guilty of the three-clan punishment.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao was warned that anyone who followed their king would die with his whole extended family.

sentences.zh: 唯田叔、孟舒等十餘人赭衣自髡鉗，隨王至長安。

translations.literal: Only Tian Shu, Meng Shu, and more than ten others in convicts’ crimson garb shaved their own heads and put on the iron collar, and followed the king to Chang’an.
translations.idiomatic: Only Tian Shu, Meng Shu, and a dozen others shaved their heads, donned the felon’s collar and crimson robe, and followed their king into captivity at Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 趙王敖事白，得出，廢王為宣平侯，乃進言叔等十人。

translations.literal: When King Ao of Zhao’s affair was cleared, he was able to come out; the king was deposed and made Marquis of Xuanping, and thereupon advanced recommendation of Shu and the ten men.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Ao was cleared of the plot, released from prison, demoted from king to Marquis of Xuanping, and then recommended Tian Shu and the other nine to the throne.

sentences.zh: 上召見，與語，漢廷臣無能出其右者。

translations.literal: The sovereign summoned and saw them, spoke with them, and among Han court ministers none could surpass them.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor called them in; after talking with them he found that none of his own ministers could match them.

sentences.zh: 上說，盡拜為郡守、諸侯相。

translations.literal: The sovereign was pleased and all were appointed commandery governors or chancellors of the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: Pleased, he gave them all posts as commandery governors or as chancellors of princely kingdoms.

sentences.zh: 叔為漢中守十餘年。

translations.literal: Shu was governor of Hanzhong for more than ten years.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu governed Hanzhong for over a decade.

sentences.zh: 孝文帝初立，召叔問曰：「公知天下長者乎？」

translations.literal: When Emperor Xiaowen had first been established, he summoned Shu and asked: “Sir, do you know who in the realm are elders of worth?”
translations.idiomatic: Early in Emperor Wen’s reign he summoned Tian Shu and asked, “Who, in your judgment, are the most honorable men in the empire?”

sentences.zh: 對曰：「臣何足以知之！」

translations.literal: He replied: “How could your servant suffice to know them!”
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu answered, “I am far too humble to say.”

sentences.zh: 上曰：「公長者，宜知之。」

translations.literal: The sovereign said: “You are an elder of worth; you ought to know.”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, “You are such a man yourself—you must have an opinion.”

sentences.zh: 叔頓首曰：「故雲中守孟舒，長者也。」

translations.literal: Shu knocked his head and said: “The former Governor of Yunzhong, Meng Shu, is an elder of worth.”
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu kowtowed and named Meng Shu, the former governor of Yunzhong.

sentences.zh: 是時孟舒坐虜大入雲中免。

translations.literal: At that time Meng Shu sat because the barbarians had entered Yunzhong in great force and was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Meng Shu had just been stripped of office after a major barbarian raid on Yunzhong.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「先帝置孟舒雲中十餘年矣，虜常一入，孟舒不能堅守，無故士卒戰死者數百人。

translations.literal: The sovereign said: “The former emperor placed Meng Shu in Yunzhong for more than ten years; the barbarians regularly entered once, Meng Shu could not firmly hold, and for no reason several hundred soldiers died in battle.
translations.idiomatic: Wen objected: “The late emperor kept Meng Shu in Yunzhong for years, yet the raiders broke in and he could not hold the line—hundreds of soldiers died, and for what?”

sentences.zh: 長者固殺人乎？」

translations.literal: Do elders of worth thus kill people?”
translations.idiomatic: Is that how a “honorable man” behaves—by getting hundreds killed?”

sentences.zh: 叔叩頭曰：「夫貫高等謀反，天子下明詔，趙有敢隨張王者罪三族，然孟舒自髡鉗，隨張王，以身死之，豈自知為雲中守哉！

translations.literal: Shu knocked his head and said: “When Guan Gao and others plotted rebellion, the Son of Heaven issued a clear edict that in Zhao whoever dared follow King Zhang would be guilty of the three-clan punishment, yet Meng Shu of himself shaved head and collar and followed King Zhang, with his body to die for him—how could he have known he would become governor of Yunzhong!
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu kowtowed again and said, “When Guan Gao conspired, the emperor decreed death for the whole clan of anyone who stayed with King Zhang Ao—yet Meng Shu shaved his head like a convict and followed his lord, ready to die with him. He had no idea he would ever govern Yunzhong!

sentences.zh: 漢與楚相距，士卒罷敝，而匈奴冒頓新服北夷，來為邊寇，孟舒知士卒罷敝，不忍出言，士爭臨城死敵，如子為父，以故死者數百人，孟舒豈敺之哉！

translations.literal: Han and Chu faced each other; the soldiers were exhausted, while the Xiongnu Modun newly subdued the northern Yi and came as border raiders; Meng Shu knew the soldiers were exhausted, could not bear to order them out, and the men vied to mount the walls and die against the foe like sons for a father—on this account several hundred died—how could Meng Shu have driven them!
translations.idiomatic: Han and Chu had worn the army to the bone, and Modu had just cowed the northern tribes and turned them on the border. Meng Shu saw how spent his men were and could not bring himself to hold them back; they rushed to the walls and died like sons defending a father. Those hundreds fell of their own zeal—Meng Shu did not drive them to it!

sentences.zh: 是乃孟舒所以為長者。」

translations.literal: This is why Meng Shu is an elder of worth.”
translations.idiomatic: That is precisely what makes Meng Shu a man of true honor.”

sentences.zh: 於是上曰：「賢哉孟舒！」

translations.literal: Thereupon the sovereign said: “How worthy is Meng Shu!”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor exclaimed, “Meng Shu is a worthy man indeed!”

sentences.zh: 復召以為雲中守。

translations.literal: He was again summoned and made governor of Yunzhong.
translations.idiomatic: He recalled Meng Shu and restored him as governor of Yunzhong.

sentences.zh: 後數歲，叔坐法失官。

translations.literal: After several years, Shu by sitting on the law lost office.
translations.idiomatic: Some years later Tian Shu lost his post over a legal matter.

sentences.zh: 梁孝王使人殺漢議臣爰盎，景帝召叔案梁，具得其事。

translations.literal: King Xiao of Liang had someone kill the Han deliberating minister Yuan Ang; Emperor Jing summoned Shu to investigate Liang, and he fully obtained the facts.
translations.idiomatic: When Prince Xiao of Liang had the Han minister Yuan Ang murdered, Emperor Jing sent Tian Shu to investigate the kingdom of Liang, and he uncovered the whole story.

sentences.zh: 還報，上曰：「梁有之乎？」

translations.literal: Returning he reported; the sovereign said: “Did Liang have it?”
translations.idiomatic: On his return the emperor asked, “Was Liang behind it?”

sentences.zh: 對曰：「有之。」

translations.literal: He replied: “There was.”
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu said, “Yes.”

sentences.zh: 「事安在？」

translations.literal: “Where is the affair?”
translations.idiomatic: “Where is the proof?”

sentences.zh: 叔曰：「上無以梁事為問也。

translations.literal: Shu said: “The sovereign should not ask about Liang’s affair.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu replied, “Your Majesty should not press for details of Liang’s guilt.

sentences.zh: 今梁王不伏誅，是廢漢法也；

translations.literal: Now if the king of Liang does not submit to execution, this is to abolish Han law;
translations.idiomatic: If the prince goes unpunished, the law of Han is a dead letter;

sentences.zh: 如其伏誅，太后食不甘味，臥不安席，此憂在陛下。」

translations.literal: if he submits to execution, the empress dowager will not find her food sweet, will not rest easy on her mat—this worry lies with Your Majesty.”
translations.idiomatic: if he is executed, the empress dowager will lose her appetite and her sleep—the grief will be yours alone.”

sentences.zh: 於是上大賢之，以為魯相。

translations.literal: Thereupon the sovereign greatly deemed him worthy and made him chancellor of Lu.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was deeply impressed and named him chancellor of Lu.

sentences.zh: 相初至官，民以王取其財物自言者百餘人。

translations.literal: When the chancellor first arrived at office, commoners who on the basis that the king had taken their goods spoke for themselves were more than a hundred.
translations.idiomatic: Soon after he took office, well over a hundred commoners came forward claiming the prince had seized their property.

sentences.zh: 叔取其渠率二千人笞，怒之曰：「王非汝主邪？

translations.literal: Shu took their chiefs, two thousand men, had them flogged, and angrily said to them: “Is not the king your lord?
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu seized their ringleaders—some two thousand men—and had them flogged, shouting, “Is the prince not your sovereign?

sentences.zh: 何敢自言主！」

translations.literal: How dare you speak of your lord on your own!”
translations.idiomatic: How dare you bring suit against your sovereign in open court!”

sentences.zh: 魯王聞之，大慚，發中府錢，使相償之。

translations.literal: The king of Lu heard it, was greatly ashamed, released money from the central treasury, and had the chancellor repay them.
translations.idiomatic: The Prince of Lu was mortified, opened the inner treasury, and told the chancellor to pay them off.

sentences.zh: 相曰：「王自使人償之，不爾，是王為惡而相為善也。」

translations.literal: The chancellor said: “Let the king himself send people to repay them; if not, this is the king doing evil and the chancellor doing good.”
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu refused: “Let His Highness send his own men with the money—otherwise it will look as though the prince did wrong and the chancellor bought virtue.”

sentences.zh: 魯王好獵，相常從入苑中，王輒休相就館。

translations.literal: The king of Lu loved hunting; the chancellor often followed into the park; the king always rested the chancellor and had him go to the lodge.
translations.idiomatic: The Prince of Lu loved the chase; the chancellor always rode with him into the hunting park, but the prince kept urging him to withdraw to the guest quarters.

sentences.zh: 相常暴坐苑外，終不休，曰：「吾王暴露，獨何為舍？」

translations.literal: The chancellor often sat exposed outside the park and in the end did not rest, saying: “My king is exposed to the elements; why alone should I lodge?”
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu would sit outside in the weather and refuse to leave, saying, “If my lord endures wind and cold, how may I take shelter alone?”

sentences.zh: 王以故不大出遊。

translations.literal: The king for this reason did not greatly go out on excursions.
translations.idiomatic: After that the prince rarely went out on long hunts.

sentences.zh: 數年以官卒，魯以百金祠，少子仁不受，曰：「義不傷先人名。」

translations.literal: After several years he died in office; Lu offered a hundred catties of gold for sacrifice; the youngest son Ren did not accept, saying: “By righteousness I will not wound the former man’s name.”
translations.idiomatic: He died in office some years later; the state of Lu sent a hundred catties of gold for his funeral, but his youngest son Ren declined it, saying, “Duty forbids me to tarnish my father’s reputation.”

sentences.zh: 仁以壯勇為衛將軍舍人，數從擊匈奴。

translations.literal: Ren by stalwart courage was a retainer of the General-in-chief Wei; several times he followed to strike the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Ren’s courage won him a place as a client of General-in-chief Wei Qing, and he rode with him on several campaigns against the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 衛將軍進言仁為郎中，至二千石、丞相長史，失官。

translations.literal: The General-in-chief advanced speech of Ren as gentleman of the palace, reaching two thousand piculs and chief clerk of the chancellor, and lost office.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Qing recommended him for gentleman of the palace; he rose to two-thousand-bushel rank and chief clerk under the chancellor, then lost his post.

sentences.zh: 後使刺三河，還，奏事稱意，拜為京輔都尉。

translations.literal: Afterward he was sent to investigate the Three Rivers; returning, his submitted affairs matched the sovereign’s mind, and he was appointed metropolitan commandant of the capital region.
translations.idiomatic: Later he was dispatched to inspect the Three Rivers circuit; his report pleased the throne, and he was named metropolitan commandant for the capital region.

sentences.zh: 月餘，遷司直。

translations.literal: After a month more, he was transferred to rectifier.
translations.idiomatic: A month later he was promoted to imperial rectifier.

sentences.zh: 數歲，戾太子舉兵，仁部閉城門，令太子得亡，坐縱反者族。

translations.literal: After several years the heir apparent Li raised troops; Ren’s division closed the city gates, letting the heir apparent obtain escape; by sitting for releasing the rebel he was clan-exterminated.
translations.idiomatic: Years afterward, when the heir apparent Liu Ju rose in arms, Ren’s detachment shut the city gates in a way that let the prince slip away; for abetting a rebel he and his clan were executed.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：以項羽之氣，而季布以勇顯名楚，身履軍搴旗者數矣，可謂壯士。

translations.literal: The eulogy says: With Xiang Yu’s spirit, yet Ji Bu by courage displayed his name in Chu, personally treading the army and seizing flags several times—he may be called a stalwart man.
translations.idiomatic: The historian’s judgment: In an age of Xiang Yu’s fury, Ji Bu made a name in Chu for sheer nerve—time and again he charged the enemy line and tore down their banners—a true fighting man.

sentences.zh: 及至困厄奴僇，苟活而不變，何也？

translations.literal: And when they reached straits of slavery and humiliation, yet clung to life without altering course—why?
translations.idiomatic: Yet when they sank to the life of a slave and the disgrace of the collar, they still clung to life instead of throwing it away—why?

sentences.zh: 彼自負其材，受辱不羞，欲有所用其未足也，故終為漢名將。

translations.literal: They themselves bore their talents; receiving shame they were not ashamed, wishing to have use for what was not yet sufficient, therefore in the end they became renowned Han generals.
translations.idiomatic: They trusted their own abilities; they could endure humiliation because they were not finished with their ambitions, and in the end they rose to fame as Han’s great generals.

sentences.zh: 賢者誠重其死。

translations.literal: The worthy truly weigh their death.
translations.idiomatic: A worthy man weighs his death as he weighs his life.

sentences.zh: 夫婢妾賤人，感概而自殺，非能勇也，其畫無俚之至耳。

translations.literal: As for maidservants and concubines, base persons, stirred by indignation and killing themselves—this cannot count as courage; their reckoning is witless in the extreme.
translations.idiomatic: When a bondmaid or some other nobody works herself into a passion and cuts her own throat, that is not bravery—it is the most witless kind of gesture.

sentences.zh: 欒布哭彭越，田叔隨張敖，赴死如歸，彼誠知所處，雖古烈士，何以加哉！

translations.literal: Luan Bu weeping for Peng Yue, Tian Shu following Zhang Ao—going to death like returning home—they truly knew their place; even ancient determined knights—how could one add to them!
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu who mourned Peng Yue, Tian Shu who followed Zhang Ao to the scaffold—they went to death as calmly as homecoming because they knew exactly where they stood; the sternest heroes of antiquity could do no more.
